definition: When USD strengthens, local-currency revenue rises for commodity producers in BRL, CLP, ZAR countries, keeping marginal supply online and capping USD commodity prices. This stabilizes commodities but perpetuates producer-currency weakness — a structural FX divergence driver that rate models entirely ignore. Example: Vale's iron ore breakeven shifts from $44/t to $40/t when BRL depreciates 15%.
evidence: Mechanical identity operating across BRL, CLP, ZAR producer currencies
Vale iron ore breakeven: $44/t to $40/t with 15% BRL depreciation
Pair_1 debate validated mechanism while noting imported inputs partially offset
Explains persistent producer-currency weakness in strong-dollar regimes that rate-based models miss
Vale iron ore AISC shifts from ~$44/t to ~$40/t when BRL depreciates 15%
Codelco copper breakeven drops from ~$2.30/lb to ~$2.00/lb with 15% CLP depreciation
Imported inputs (20-30% of AISC) partially offset but do not neutralize the effect
open_questions: How large is the imported-input offset for different commodities?
Does this mechanism create exploitable mean-reversion in producer-currency pairs?
Whether the biological lag distinction creates exploitable timing differences between energy/metals exporters (AUD, CLP, NOK) and agricultural exporters (BRL, ARS)
